revision.text:
'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。経では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、伝では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを四象といい、それぞれ老陽（陽-陽）、少陰（陽-陰）、少陽（陰-陽）、老陰（陰-陰）と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、最初の字は爻の順序を表し、2字目は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示すものを書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。
[[Category:易|こう]]

revision.comment: 爻題の順序、また無用なリンクをはずす
revision.text:
'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものは四象といい、それぞれ老陽（陽-陽）、少陰（陽-陰）、少陽（陰-陽）、老陰（陰-陰）と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]] [[zh:爻]]

'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを四象といい、それぞれ老陽（陽-陽）、少陰（陽-陰）、少陽（陰-陽）、老陰（陰-陰）と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]] [[zh:爻]]

'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ[[画像:Taiyang.png]]太陽・[[画像:Shaoyin.png]]少陰・[[画像:Shaoyang_4.png]]少陽・[[画像:Taiyin.png]]太陰と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]] [[zh:爻]]

{{占い}} '''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ[[画像:Taiyang.png]]太陽・[[画像:Shaoyin.png]]少陰・[[画像:Shaoyang_4.png]]少陽・[[画像:Taiyin.png]]太陰と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。

revision.comment: ロボットによる 追加: [[uk:Ключ 89]]
revision.text:
'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ[[画像:Taiyang.png]]太陽・[[画像:Shaoyin.png]]少陰・[[画像:Shaoyang_4.png]]少陽・[[画像:Taiyin.png]]太陰と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]]
[[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]] [[uk:Ключ 89]] [[zh:爻]]

'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ[[画像:Taiyang.png]]太陽・[[画像:Shaoyin.png]]少陰・[[画像:Shaoyang_4.png]]少陽・[[画像:Taiyin.png]]太陰と呼ぶ。
経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。そして爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]]
[[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]] [[zh:爻]]

revision.comment: ベタ書きだった文章を，項目に分けて体系化しました．
revision.text:
'''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（─）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（--）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。
== 意味 == 陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。
これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。
なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ[[画像:Taiyang.png]]太陽・[[画像:Shaoyin.png]]少陰・[[画像:Shaoyang_4.png]]少陽・[[画像:Taiyin.png]]太陰と呼ぶ。
== 爻題と爻辞 == 経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。
=== 爻題 === 爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。
一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。
=== 爻辞 === 爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。
例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。
なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
[[Category:易|こう]]

revision.comment: Created page with "{{distinguish|Radical 89}}"
revision.text: {{distinguish|Radical 89}}

{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023年8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|ja|易}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

revision.text:
{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|ja|易}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=draft}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=draft}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦|12=draft}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦|12=draft}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|12=draft}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学|12=draft}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=draft}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=draft}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|12=draft}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!![[Unicode]]!![[JIS X 0213]]!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}

=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|12=draft|12=draft}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

revision.text:
{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|12=draft|12=draft}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=draft|12=draft}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=draft|12=draft}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦|12=draft|12=draft}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦|12=draft|12=draft}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|12=draft|12=draft}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学|12=draft|12=draft}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=draft|12=draft}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=draft|12=draft}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|12=draft|12=draft}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!![[Unicode]]!![[JIS X 0213]]!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}

revision.comment: 20 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:爻 up to rev 96652957
revision.text:
{{出典の明記| date = 2023年8月}} {{for|漢字の部首|爻部}} '''爻'''（こう）は、[[易]]の[[卦]]を構成する基本記号。長い横棒（{{large|⚊}}）と真ん中が途切れた2つの短い横棒（{{large|⚋}}）の2種類がある。[[経]]では前者を'''剛'''、後者を'''柔'''と呼ぶが、[[伝]]では'''陽'''、'''陰'''とする。
== 意味 == 陽爻と陰爻は対立する二面性を表し、陽爻は男性・積極性などを、陰爻は女性・消極性などを表す。
これらを3つ組み合わせた三爻により[[八卦]]ができ、六爻により[[六十四卦]]が作られる。このように陽爻と陰爻を組み合わせることにより事物のさまざまな側面を説明する。
なお[[朱子学]]系統の易学、宋易において爻を2つ組み合わせてできるものを[[四象 (易)|四象]]といい、それぞれ{{large|⚌}}太陽・{{large|⚍}}少陰・{{large|⚎}}少陽・{{large|⚏}}太陰と呼ぶ。
== 爻題と爻辞 == 経には六十四卦の爻ごとに爻題と爻辞が付されている。
=== 爻題 === 爻題は必ず2字で構成され、1字は爻の順序を表し、もう1字は爻の性質、すなわち剛（陽）か柔（陰）かを表す。爻の順序は下から上であり、1番下の爻から「初・二・三・四・五・上」と名付けられる。
一方、爻の性質は数字によって表され陽爻には九、陰爻には六が用いられる。
=== 爻辞 === 爻辞にはそれぞれの爻が示す[[占い]]の言葉を書き記している。
例えば、[[乾]]卦の初爻であれば、「初九、潜龍勿用」というように書かれており、「初九」が爻題、「潜龍勿用」が爻辞である。
なお爻題は初爻と上爻では順位・性質の順番であるが、二爻から五爻では性質・順位の順になる（例えば六二、九四というように）。
== 符号位置 == Yijing monogram symbols {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !記号!![[Unicode]]!![[JIS X 0213]]!![[文字参照]]!!名称 {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YING}} |} [[Category:易|こう]]

=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|qq=draft|qq=draft}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

revision.text:
{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|qq=draft|qq=draft}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|qq=draft|qq=draft}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学|qq=draft|qq=draft}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|qq=draft|qq=draft}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!![[Unicode]]!![[JIS X 0213]]!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}

Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

revision.text:
{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/I Ching|qq=}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|qq=}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|qq=}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Eight Trigrams|ja|八卦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Eight Trigrams|qq=}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Sixty-Four Hexagrams|ja|六十四卦|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sixty-Four Hexagrams|qq=}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|ja|易|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/I Ching|qq=}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Zhu Xi|ja|朱子学|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Zhu Xi|qq=}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|qq=}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|qq=}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qian (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|qq=}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|qq=}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qian (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

revision.text:
{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4538410]] Wikidata name: yao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{出典の明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|qq=|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|qq=|1=Den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|qq=|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|qq=|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:爻]][[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]][[uk:Яо (риска)]][[zh:爻]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4538410]] Wikidata name: yao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{Referencesの明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|1=Den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!![[:en:Unicode|Unicode]]!![[:en:JIS X 0213|JIS X 0213]]!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}
{{translated page|ja|爻|version=96652957|comment=Imported full ja history}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:I Ching]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }}

In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).

=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.

Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==

By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.

In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).

=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|1=Den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the [[:en:Bagua|Eight Trigrams]] (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the [[:en:Hexagram (I Ching)|Sixty-Four Hexagrams]] (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty [[:en:I Ching|I Ching]] studies, particularly those following the [[:en:Cheng–Zhu school|Zhu Xi]] school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!![[:en:Unicode|Unicode]]!![[:en:JIS X 0213|JIS X 0213]]!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}

In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).

For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:爻]][[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]][[uk:Яо (риска)]][[zh:爻]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:爻]][[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]][[uk:Яо (риска)]][[zh:爻]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:爻]][[ru:Яо (гексаграмма)]][[uk:Яо (риска)]][[zh:爻]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q4538410]] Wikidata name: yao [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:28, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{Referencesの明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|1=Den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Eight Trigrams}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Hexagram (I Ching)|en|Hexagram (I Ching)|WD=Q1572403|lt=Sixty-Four Hexagrams}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Cheng–Zhu school|en|Cheng–Zhu school|WD=Q841735|lt=Zhu Xi}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!!{{ill|Unicode|en|Unicode|WD=Q8819|lt=Unicode}}!!{{ill|JIS X 0213|en|JIS X 0213|WD=Q6108269|lt=JIS X 0213}}!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}
{{translated page|ja|爻|version=96652957|comment=Imported full ja history}}

=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

{{distinguish|Radical 89}} {{Referencesの明記| date = 2023rd year 8月}} '''Kō''' (爻) is a fundamental symbol that composes the {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching}} hexagrams. There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|1=Den|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).
== Meaning ==
Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Eight Trigrams}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Hexagram (I Ching)|en|Hexagram (I Ching)|WD=Q1572403|lt=Sixty-Four Hexagrams}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Cheng–Zhu school|en|Cheng–Zhu school|WD=Q841735|lt=Zhu Xi}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Four Symbols (I Ching)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|ltq=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Eight Trigrams|1=Bagua}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Hexagram (I Ching)|en|Hexagram (I Ching)|WD=Q1572403|lt=Sixty-Four Hexagrams|1=Hexagram (I Ching)}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching|1=I Ching}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Cheng–Zhu school|en|Cheng–Zhu school|WD=Q841735|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Cheng–Zhu school}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Q11420141|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).
== Kōdai and Kōji ==
In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).
=== Kōdai ===
The *kōdai* consists of two characters: one indicating the position of the line within the hexagram, and the other denoting its nature—whether it is yang (*gō*, 剛) or yin (*jū*, 柔).
The positions of the lines are numbered from the bottom upwards and are named: **Shō (初), Ni (二), San (三), Shi (四), Go (五), and Jō (上).**
Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.
=== Kōji ===
The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).
== Encoding in Unicode ==
Yijing monogram symbols: {| class="wikitable" style="text-align:center;" !Symbol!!{{ill|Unicode|en|Unicode|WD=Q8819|lt=Unicode|1=Unicode}}!!{{ill|JIS X 0213|en|JIS X 0213|WD=Q6108269|lt=JIS X 0213|1=JIS X 0213}}!![[Character Reference]]!!Name {{CharCode|9866|268A||MONOGRAM FOR YANG}} {{CharCode|9867|268B||MONOGRAM FOR YIN}} |}

There are two types: a long horizontal line ({{large|⚊}}) and a broken horizontal line consisting of two short segments ({{large|⚋}}). In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, the former is called '''gō''' (剛) and the latter '''jū''' (柔), but in the {{ill|Den|ja|伝|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Den|1=Q11381354|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11381354}}, they are referred to as '''yō''' (陽) and '''in''' (陰).

Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) and yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) represent opposing dualities. A yang line symbolizes masculinity, activity, and assertiveness, whereas a yin line signifies femininity, passivity, and receptivity.
By combining three such lines, the {{ill|Bagua|en|Bagua|WD=Q340846|lt=Eight Trigrams|1=Bagua}} (*bagua*) are formed, and by combining six lines, the {{ill|Hexagram (I Ching)|en|Hexagram (I Ching)|WD=Q1572403|lt=Sixty-Four Hexagrams|1=Hexagram (I Ching)}} (*liushisi gua*) are created. In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching|1=I Ching}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Cheng–Zhu school|en|Cheng–Zhu school|WD=Q841735|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Cheng–Zhu school}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Q11420141|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).

In the {{ill|Jing|ja|経|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jing|1=經|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1305383}}, each of the sixty-four hexagrams' lines (*kō*) is assigned a *kōdai* (爻題) and a *kōji* (爻辞).

Meanwhile, the nature of the lines is represented by numbers: - Yang lines (*yō-kō*, 陽爻) are indicated by **nine (九)**. - Yin lines (*in-kō*, 陰爻) are indicated by **six (六)**.

The *kōji* (爻辞) records the divinatory meaning associated with each line.
For example, in the {{ill|Qián (I Ching)|ja|乾|1=Qián (Bagua)|2=ja|3=乾|lt=Qian (乾)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qián (I Ching)|qq=}} hexagram, the first line (*shō-kō*, 初爻) is written as: **"Shō-kyū, Senryū motsuyō"** (初九、潜龍勿用). Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
Notably, in the first (初) and top (上) positions, the order of notation follows **position → nature** (e.g., 初九), whereas in the second through fifth positions, the order reverses to **nature → position** (e.g., 六二, 九四).

In this way, various aspects of phenomena are explained by the combination of yang and yin lines.
Additionally, in Song-dynasty {{ill|I Ching|en|I Ching|WD=Q181937|lt=I Ching|1=I Ching}} studies, particularly those following the {{ill|Cheng–Zhu school|en|Cheng–Zhu school|WD=Q841735|lt=Zhu Xi|1=Cheng–Zhu school}} school, combining two lines results in what is known as the {{ill|Four Symbols (I Ching)|ja|四象 (易)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Symbols (I Ching)|1=Q11420141|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11420141}} (*shishō*), which are classified as: {{large|⚌}} *Great Yang* (太陽), {{large|⚍}} *Lesser Yin* (少陰), {{large|⚎}} *Lesser Yang* (少陽), {{large|⚏}} *Great Yin* (太陰).

Here, "Shō-kyū" (初九) is the *kōdai*, while "Senryū motsuyō" (潜龍勿用) is the *kōji*.
